chapters.title: Why our casual attitude toward voting hurts us more than it hurts politicians
chapters.text:
Many people give up on elections or treat them like a chore they would rather skip. They feel frustrated by the process, or they assume their vote cannot change anything. Even so, the choice to vote belongs to each of us, and the lack of interest often comes from the tactics powerful groups use to wear people down.

Voter suppression is a strategy that aims to discourage people, especially people of color, from casting a ballot. The ruling class benefits when turnout drops. Marginalized communities tend to push hardest for fair governance and real representation. In response, they face obstacles like election violence, harassment, and confusing registration rules designed to push them away from the polls.

The fear of voting, and the feeling that our votes do not matter, has caused serious harm. When we sit out an election, we hand our future to people who want to keep the system tilted in their favor. Suppression plants doubt in our minds, and it is up to us to pull up those weeds before they spread.

Stacey Abrams ran for governor in Georgia and lost, but she has spent years explaining how suppression works at the federal and state level. If you want to understand how voter suppression endangers democracy, this summary walks through her view.

As Stacey Abrams has written, voting is a constitutional right in the United States, a right reaffirmed by three separate amendments.

chapters.title: Voter suppression is a strategy governments use to block people from exercising their rights
chapters.text:
After the Jim Crow voting laws were dismantled in 1965, Black and brown Americans were finally allowed to register and cast ballots. The Voting Rights Act was a major milestone in Black history, and at the time it felt almost too good to be true.

Stacey Abrams's grandmother, Wilter, once described the moment to her. She admitted she was too afraid to walk to the polling station. The violence she had seen at the hands of white supremacists was still fresh in her memory. Black Americans had been treated with contempt and stripped of basic rights. Police officers beat Black people and set dogs on them. That level of brutality left many Black families afraid, so afraid that freedom itself seemed like a rumor.

In the United States today, Black voters still run into barriers in registration and at the polls. In Georgia, more than 80 percent of voters whose registrations hit problems were people of color. The point is not a paperwork mix-up. The point is to make voting feel pointless.

Violence and rigged elections also send a message that votes do not matter, so why bother turning out?

When Stacey Abrams ran for governor in 2018, she made voter suppression a central issue. She traveled the state and made the case that people needed to fight for their right to vote. She lost to Brian Kemp, but turnout in that election was higher than any governor's race in Georgia's history. More people decided that staying home would not change anything.

As a citizen, your vote is your right, and it is the power that decides your fate and the fate of the next generation.

chapters.title: Complicated registration rules are designed to keep people from getting an ID and voting
chapters.text:
The first move in a suppression campaign is to make registration difficult. If someone manages to register anyway, the next step is to make the actual vote nearly impossible. And if a person does vote, the rules are written so that their ballot barely counts.

The government can use many tricks to slow registration down. Beyond claiming a voter is ineligible on a flimsy pretext, officials sometimes add small errors to a person's details so the ballot cannot be cast.

Black voters have long been the main target, but the same tactics are now aimed at Latino voters. In Ford County, Kansas, about 28,000 people were blocked from voting because the county closed its only polling place and moved it to the edge of town. Most of those residents tend to vote Democratic, and limiting their access was the point.

Ballot counting is another pressure point. In the days before the 2018 Georgia governor's race, Black voters reported being visited at home, questioned about where they lived, and pressured in other ways. Stacey Abrams herself showed up at her polling station and was told she had already voted by absentee ballot. She had not requested an absentee ballot. A few minutes later, she was told there had been an error and she could vote in person. Stacey knew her rights and had the standing to make noise at the polling station. That raises an obvious question. What happens to voters who do not have that kind of influence? Most of the people who had the same problem were people of color.

It is easy to lose interest in something when you realize that your last effort did not count.

The best way to make sure our votes matter is to stand on our rights and demand full transparency.

chapters.title: Broken voting systems and rigged politics must be replaced with inclusive constitutional rules
chapters.text:
In August 2019, a fourteen-second video went viral during a runoff primary in Georgia. A voter shared his experience at the machine. He tapped the screen for one candidate, but the machine kept registering a different choice. After the clip spread, other voters came forward with similar stories about the machines selecting candidates they did not pick.

Voting machines run on software, and software is written by people. Even if election officials insist nothing was rigged, it strains belief that the equipment just happened to favor one candidate.

The system rarely gives the minority a fair shake. Long lines in opposition neighborhoods, broken machines near elderly voters, intimidation, and threats of violence all work to keep people away from the polls. Teaching voters about these tricks is more urgent than ever. There is no other way to say it. If we do not vote, we lose our voice in our own future.

We have to remember that, together, we can challenge the status quo when we are loud and clear about what we want.

Recent turnout numbers from 2018 and 2019 show real improvement. The ruling class now knows it will face scrutiny, but the work is not finished. We can protect our votes by checking them carefully on the screen before we walk away. We can also cut down on chaos by arriving early and being patient with the line. As much as we want change, much of the responsibility still sits with the government. Unless officials take real steps to register people cleanly and protect their ballots, many voters will keep feeling pushed out and may stop showing up altogether.

A healthy democracy gives every candidate a fair field and gives voters real reason to believe their ballots matter. Once voters trust that their ballots shape the outcome, they will turn out in large numbers to back their choice.

chapters.title: Citizenship demands action that moves us past danger and toward real democracy
chapters.text:
Citizenship means more than filling out a form. It means voting, protesting, and showing up. It means fighting on every front so the next generation does not have to live under suppression. Our right to vote is not just about choosing leaders. It shapes our present and our future. Not everyone wants the same thing or shares the same ideas. Each person has a preferred candidate, and each person has reasons for that choice. To help your candidate win, you have to show up and vote.

Good leadership should always center representation. Voters have the right to pick someone who speaks for them, and the people we elect have to remember that they answer to us. When we skip an election, we let someone who does not share our views speak in our place.

Voter suppression usually targets the same communities that push hardest for a fair society.

If we do not fight to make our votes count, we stay on the margins, unheard and misrepresented at the highest levels.

To fight suppression effectively, we have to set a floor for democracy. Every citizen over 18 should be registered automatically. The process of getting on the rolls should be quick and simple. The faster and easier registration is, the more people sign up. There should also be pre-registration for sixteen and seventeen year olds, so when they turn eighteen their records are already updated and they only need to pick up their voter card.

Voters should not be turned away because of strict ID rules. Any form of identification that proves citizenship and eligibility should be enough.

One thing worth noting is that, after Stacey Abrams and her team ran their registration campaign in 2018, more than 1.4 million returning citizens with past felony convictions regained the right to vote.

chapters.title: Identity politics shapes our decisions and can push our lives in a better direction
chapters.text:
When Kimaya married her husband in 2007, she had no idea what was coming. Between their first meeting and the wedding, she had already experienced domestic abuse. When she threatened to leave with their daughter, he pulled out a gun and shot at both of them. Kimaya survived, but her little girl did not. It took months before the girl could speak again or feed herself.

Kimaya's story is tragic, and it is far from unique. Weak gun laws and a system that lets dangerous people slip through have caused similar pain for many American families. When Kimaya later joined politics, she did not start with a party label. Her goal was to back candidates who would pass stricter gun laws and make the country safer. Preferred candidates may change once they are in office, so it helps to build a system where no single person has the power to destabilize the country on their own. We have to elect a wide mix of citizens from every background so the top leader cannot rely on a circle of yes-men.

The gap between rich and poor demands real attention if we want to confront oppression. With enough money, elections can be stolen, and people can be suppressed quickly and violently because most voters cannot afford to live comfortably. Powerful people often prey on the weak. We have to refuse the crumbs we are handed and find a way to stand up for ourselves. We have to see past the racial and religious lines that propagandists draw. Sorting voters by skin color only drives us further apart.

As long as we cannot agree among ourselves, the government will keep picking who it wants in office and freezing out the rest.

chapters.title: Adequate representation depends on accurate counts, and American history is missing them
chapters.text:
Federal law requires a census every ten years to count everyone living in the United States and to give the government a sense of how many people it serves. The census runs on schedule, but it has been used as a tool to shape the count in ways that benefit the ruling class. The procedures meant to keep the count accurate have been bent by people who want to make sure people of color are undercounted. The damage is not limited to voter suppression. When the count is wrong, the communities that were missed also lose out on funding and services they are owed. If the government counts 10 million people in a state of 14 million, at least 4 million residents will be underrepresented.

We have to push for reform that lets the government register people through automatic ID systems with fingerprint records, so registration runs smoothly.

The presidential electoral system should also be rebuilt. In the last presidential election, the winner took the White House despite losing the popular vote by more than 3 million ballots. States need to agree that the candidate with the most votes wins. Then voters in states with smaller electoral weight will not feel ignored.

To win for ourselves and our preferred candidates, we need to do three things.

First, reject the myths about who votes and why. Do not get distracted by stories about which voters support which candidate. The press will sometimes push a false picture that one candidate is destined to win because of who backs them. Stay focused on your candidate and your vote.

Second, move early. Capture the attention of the voters you want by sharing your plans and giving them a clear sense of what is coming.

Third, build a loyal team. The best people to keep close during a campaign are those who stay loyal through thick and thin.

If we can put these three ideas into practice together, we are on our way to making real change.

chapters.title: Populism, when left unchecked, slides toward authoritarianism
chapters.text:
The Democratic Party was built in part on the idea of populism. Populism calls for the representation of people who have been pushed to the margins. The goal is right, but the movement keeps getting hijacked by people who see it as a route to power. Some figures will infiltrate the movement, take it over, and rebrand it as an authoritarian project where voters have almost no say. History has plenty of examples of rebel leaders who overthrew a tyrant and then ruled even more harshly than the leader they replaced. Today, populism is doing more harm than good and is straining democracies across the world. In our search for inclusive politics, we have to watch out for opportunists with a hidden agenda.

According to the annual Democracy Index, the United States has fallen to number 25 among the world's working democracies. Our standing is being worn down by the politics of deceit practiced by our leaders. In the present-day United States, leaders rarely consult voters before making choices that affect everyone.

Authoritarian populism has pulled power away from voters and placed it in the hands of opportunists who do not really care about the public. The standoff over immigration policy, for instance, has left many children separated from their families. We have to take power back, and the cleanest way to do it is through the ballot box. The future of our democracy depends on how much power we are willing to hand to our leaders.

Other countries are also feeling strain on their democratic institutions. China's posture is putting pressure on Taiwan's democracy, and Russia continues to attack Ukraine for political gain. The promise of freedom that wealthy nations offer developing countries can be a cynical move dressed up as international partnership. Many countries have suffered major losses after taking help from people whose real goal was to loot them.

To repair America's damaged image, we have to elect a president who stands for everyone, regardless of skin color or religion.

We have to strengthen our democracy and clear away every barrier in our path. Otherwise, we will keep living through the same troubles, only worse.

chapters.title: The next version of America must welcome people of every race, religion, and orientation
chapters.text:
Brian Kemp, Stacey's main opponent, promised to push the LGBTQ+ community out of Georgia and give preference to what he called the true people of the state. Looking at the pattern of governor's races in Georgia, it is clear that oppression has often won. That is because we have not pushed hard enough. We are making progress, but we are not there yet. The America we want is possible if we are willing to demand it.

With real commitment, the best version of America is within reach. We are closer than ever to the country of our dreams. We have to make sure the LGBTQ+ community is accepted, because no one should be punished for who they are. Our willingness to fight oppression will decide how long we wait before we see that better country.

Each of us has a moral and political duty to stand up for democracy. The next America has to reflect the real meaning of democracy, a government of the people. The country has long called itself the leader of the free world, but it has not lived up to that title. A truly free society has no room for oppression or racism, and it gives everyone a voice.

The America of tomorrow is up to us. We have to be the future and the pioneers of a new era in the United States. We have to see leaders as representatives, not as all-powerful figures whose orders we follow without question.

The Democratic Party tends to be closer to the priorities of marginalized voters than the Republican Party. To limit the Democratic vote, the GOP has used a range of tactics to frustrate those voters. If we sit this out and accept the situation as it is, we will keep losing.

We have to push back against the oppression placed on us. If we do not stand up for our rights, we will never make a difference.

Stacey Abrams has written that we are strongest when we see the most vulnerable in our society, witness their struggles, and then build systems to make life better.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The moment to make a difference is here, and we have to use it well. The decision to build a healthy country that welcomes people of every color to live under one flag without segregation is the real American dream. The power to shift our broken politics rests on our shoulders. We have to vote, and we have to make sure our votes count.

American politics is tangled up in a long history of lies, deceit, and racial prejudice. We have been sorted into groups so large that unity can feel like a lost cause, but we cannot stop. We cannot be discouraged in the face of oppression, and we have to be loud and active in the fight for a better democracy.

America's democratic credibility is slipping around the world, so we have to come together and repair it. If we do not act, we will lose our democracy and slip into a darker political climate shaped by dictatorship and authoritarianism. We will lose our sense of self and become a country where the system can be bypassed by anyone with enough money to buy a gun and cause harm.

If we want real change, our time is now. The next presidential election in 2020 is the moment to show what we can do together and prove that the United States is still a country of free people, open to anyone willing to work and build a life.

A useful step is to join the campaign for quick and clean registration and a better voting process. Lend your support to local campaign groups and teach the people around you about why voting matters and what is at stake when people stay home.
